The application is a Blood Bank Inventory Manager.
The application is a medical inventory system.
The application tracks blood unit stock in a hospital blood bank.
The application manages compatibility between blood types.
The application monitors expiration.
The application raises alerts during shortages.
The application supports safe and timely transfusions.
The application tracks blood units by ABO type.
The application tracks blood units by Rh factor.
The application tracks blood units by collection date.
The application sets expiry fixed at 42 days after collection.
The application accepts transfusion requests.
Transfusion requests specify blood type.
Transfusion requests specify Rh factor.
Transfusion requests specify quantity.
The application matches requests to compatible units.
The matching uses standard ABO/Rh donor compatibility.
The matching uses exact-type matches first.
The matching uses closest expiry first.
The application reserves matched units.
The application releases a reservation back to stock if it is not confirmed as issued within 24 hours.
The application automatically expires units past their expiry date.
The application excludes expired units from matching.
The application raises a shortage alert whenever available stock of any blood type falls below 5 units.
The application shows a dashboard of stock per type.
The application shows a dashboard of units expiring within 7 days.
The application shows a dashboard of open requests.
The system records each blood unit's ABO group, Rh factor, and the date it was collected.
Each blood donation is logged with its blood type, Rhesus factor, and collection timestamp.
A user can enter the ABO type, Rh factor, and collection date for every new blood unit added to inventory.
When a unit is registered, its ABO classification, Rh status, and harvest date are stored for later tracking.
The application captures the blood group, Rh type, and collection date of every unit to support inventory management.
The system accepts a request that specifies the desired ABO group, Rh factor, and number of units needed.
A clinician can submit a transfusion order indicating the blood type, Rh requirement, and quantity.
Transfusion requests are recorded with their blood group, Rh specification, and requested unit count.
The software processes incoming orders for blood by noting the ABO type, Rh factor, and amount required.
A request for blood products is entered into the system with the blood type, Rh, and volume desired.
The matching algorithm finds compatible inventory units based on standard ABO and Rh donor rules, prioritizing exact matches then closest expiry.
When a request comes in, the system selects suitable blood units by checking ABO/Rh compatibility and picking the one expiring soonest among exact matches.
Users see that the system pairs transfusion orders with the most appropriate available blood units using compatibility guidelines and expiry order.
The outcome of matching is a list of qualified units that are compatible with the patient's blood type and have the earliest expiration dates.
Compatible units are identified by comparing ABO and Rh factors, then sorted by expiry date to select the soonest-expiring exact match first.
The system reserves matched units for a request and automatically cancels the reservation if the units are not confirmed as issued within 24 hours.
Reserved blood units are set aside for a specific order, and if not dispensed in a day, the hold is released back to general stock.
A user can reserve compatible units for a transfusion, and the reservation expires automatically after 24 hours of non-use.
When a reservation is not fulfilled within 24 hours, the software returns those units to the available inventory pool.
The application holds units for a pending issue and reverts the hold to available stock after one day without confirmation.
Blood units that have passed their 42-day shelf life are automatically marked as expired and removed from matching consideration.
The system identifies inventory items whose expiration date has passed and excludes them from future transfusion matching.
Users do not need to manually remove outdated units; the software automatically expires them and hides them from availability.
After a unit's expiration date, it is no longer considered for any transfusion requests.
Past-due blood products are automatically flagged as expired and are not included in compatibility searches.
A shortage alert is triggered whenever the available stock for any blood type falls below five units.
If the count of any ABO/Rh combination drops under five, the system sends an alert about the low supply.
Users receive a notification when the inventory of a specific blood type becomes critically low, below five units.
The outcome is that the software warns staff about impending shortages when stock of a type is less than five.
An alarm is raised for any blood group whose current quantity is fewer than five units, indicating a potential deficit.
The dashboard displays current stock levels per blood type, units expiring in the next seven days, and any open transfusion requests.
A summary screen shows how many units of each ABO/Rh type are on hand, which ones will expire soon, and pending orders.
Users can view a consolidated panel listing inventory counts, soon-to-expire units, and unfulfilled requests.
The system presents a real-time overview of stock by type, upcoming expirations within a week, and active orders.
A monitoring interface provides a snapshot of available blood by group, near-expiry items, and outstanding transfusion requests.
